story:
Karen clutched a tattered red book and said this was more than a midlife crisis. Peter spent all his time in the garage, “doing God knows what.” Their ten-year-old, Robert, chirped that Dad was building a rocket to go back home—Neverland, where you stayed young forever. Tina, Karen’s leopard-heel-wearing best friend, laughed till Karen snapped. Tears came. Karen feared Peter was cheating; she’d seen “Wendy” flash on his phone, and Wendy was in that stupid red book too. Tina tried to soothe her with a crude tale about her own Barry’s ill‑advised “snake” tattoo, but Karen only cried harder.

They rifled Peter’s jacket and found his phone. Wendy had three missed calls. “Call her,” Tina said. Karen did. A butler answered: “The Henry Residence.” Wendy’s voice was soft and baffled. No, she wasn’t sleeping with Peter; he was an old friend having a rough time. Maybe, she suggested, Karen should listen to him—his childhood hadn’t been like most. Karen bristled at talk of Lego rockets and a light called Tink, then faltered, apologized, and hung up. She told Tina she needed to talk to Peter and sent her home across the road.

The house shook with a blast. Smoke rolled under the garage door. Karen ran straight into the heat and pulled two unmoving legs out from behind a car-sized rocket. “Robert, call 999!” she yelled, dragging Peter into the utility room as black smoke swallowed the house. She sagged, choking, until an “angel” in leopard print—Tina, hair still in rollers—slapped her awake. Barry hauled Peter. Sirens wailed. Karen fainted to the sound of neighbors clapping as they were pulled clear.

In hospital, Robert wheeled her to “Peter’s” curtained bed, where a fully bandaged figure lay suspended by wires. She wept into his chest—until a hand touched her shoulder. The real Peter sat beside her in a wheelchair, slightly charred, ears a touch pointed, eyes warm and brown. They kissed; Robert groaned, embarrassed. Peter called himself an idiot. Karen tried to hush him, but he insisted: he’d used baby powder instead of pixie dust because he and Tink had argued. Karen rolled her eyes—still the same madness. He squeezed her hands and said the only thing he was crazy about was her. He’d even cushioned the rocket’s seats for her bad back.

“Are we building another rocket to get to Neverland?” Robert asked. “Neverland isn’t real,” Karen snapped. “And Tinkerbell’s a light bulb.” She snatched the small box from Peter’s lap, ignoring his warning, and popped the lid. A faint glow breathed across her face. The room seemed to tilt toward wonder. Karen’s voice softened. “Where did you say Neverland is?”

Peter pointed toward the window, where morning would someday break. “Second star to the right,” he said, “and straight on till morning.”

Between Tina’s tart one-liners, a frightened boy’s faith, and a woman who walked through fire, the crisis wasn’t about an affair after all. It was about a man trying to stitch together a lost boy’s past with a husband’s present—and a family learning that love can be both the thing that keeps you grounded and the only real way to fly.
